coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical preparation of thin-layered molybdenum disulfide-poly(m-aminobenzenesulfonic acid) nanocomposite for TNT detection
coredata.prism:issueName: Special issue in honor of Chinese Academician Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen for his 80th birthday
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Tao
Yu, Renzhong
Chen, Huaiyin
Yang, Ruirui
Wang, Shiying
Luo, Xiliang
Jiao, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Recently, molybdenum disulfide-based nanocomposites have been widely used in electrochemical sensing area. In this paper, molybdenum disulfide-poly(m-aminobenzenesulfonic acid) (MoS2-PABSA) nanocomposite was prepared through a simple electropolymerization procedure for TNT detection. The pre-obtained thin-layered MoS2 nanosheet was adopted as the scaffold in the electropolymerization process and the m-aminobenzenesulfonic acid monomer was effectively electrodeposited on it via cyclic voltammetry. The morphology and electrochemical behaviors of MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite were investigated by scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction and some electrochemical methods. The obtained nanocomposite possesses excellent electrochemical detecting performance towards aromatic ring compounds, such as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene, TNT, which have strong π-π interaction with the MoS2-PABSA sensing platform. The detailed electrochemical characterization of MoS2-PABSA showed the influence of electropolymerization cycles and pH values on detection of TNT. Under the optimum condition, highly sensitive detection for TNT was realized with the limit of detection of 0.043ppb (0.187nmolL−1) and a linear range of 0.1–200ppb. This research proves that the MoS2-PABSA was a worthy electrochemical sensing platform for further simple and sensitive detection of some other aromatic ring compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin-layered MoS2
m-Aminobenzenesulfonic acid
TNT detection
Cyclic voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials characterization of the MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite
Effect of pH value on the reduction signal of TNT
The detection of a series of concentration of TNT
Anti-interferring ability of MoS2-PABSA and TNT detection in real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275084 , 41476083 , 21675092 , 21422504 ), Outstanding Youth Foundation of Shandong Province ( JQ201406 ), Outstanding Adult-Young Scientific Research Encouraging Foundation of Shandong Province (No. BS2012CL013 ) and the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province, China ( ts20110829 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electropolymerization process of PABSA by CV at the different electrodes: GCE (a), MoS2/GCE (b).
SEM images of MoS2 (A), PABSA (C), and MoS2-PABSA (D). TEM image of MoS2 (B).

Representative CVs (A) and Nyquist diagrams (B) of 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1:1) containing 0.1molL−1 KCl; CVs (C) in 0.30molL−1 PBS (pH7.0). DPVs (D) of 1.0ppm TNT in PBS (pH6.0) at MoS2/GCE (a), PABSA/GCE (b), MoS2-PABSA/GCE (c); Mechanism of three independent TNT reduction peaks (E).

Representative CVs (A) and Nyquist diagrams (B) of 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1:1) containing 0.1molL−1 KCl; DPVs (C) in PBS solution with 1.0ppm TNT recorded at MoS2-PABSA/GCE with different electropolymerization cycles: (a) 13cycles, (b) 14cycles, (c) 15cycles.
The effect of pH value of PBS on the electroreduction of TNT: (a) 4.0, (b) 5.0, (c) 6.0, (d) 7.0.

(A) DPVs of MoS2-PABSA/GCE in 0.3molL−1 PBS (pH6.0) with different TNT concentrations (a–j: 0.1, 1, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, and 1000ppb). (B) Plot of the peak current at −0.336V for TNT concentrations. (C) Linear relationship between peak current and TNT concentration (0.1–200ppb). Note: Error bars are the standard deviation of three repetitive experiments.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Paper dedicated to Prof. Hong-Yuan Chen of the director of Institute of Analytical Science and Institute of Chemical Biology at Nanjing University on the occasion of his 80th birthday.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical preparation of thin-layered molybdenum disulfide-poly(m-aminobenzenesulfonic acid) nanocomposite for TNT detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker of Education Ministry, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker of Education Ministry
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, molybdenum disulfide-based nanocomposites have been widely used in electrochemical sensing area. In this paper, molybdenum disulfide-poly(m-aminobenzenesulfonic acid) (MoS2-PABSA) nanocomposite was prepared through a simple electropolymerization procedure for TNT detection. The pre-obtained thin-layered MoS2 nanosheet was adopted as the scaffold in the electropolymerization process and the m-aminobenzenesulfonic acid monomer was effectively electrodeposited on it via cyclic voltammetry. The morphology and electrochemical behaviors of MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite were investigated by scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction and some electrochemical methods. The obtained nanocomposite possesses excellent electrochemical detecting performance towards aromatic ring compounds, such as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene, TNT, which have strong π-π interaction with the MoS2-PABSA sensing platform. The detailed electrochemical characterization of MoS2-PABSA showed the influence of electropolymerization cycles and pH values on detection of TNT. Under the optimum condition, highly sensitive detection for TNT was realized with the limit of detection of 0.043ppb (0.187nmolL−1) and a linear range of 0.1–200ppb. This research proves that the MoS2-PABSA was a worthy electrochemical sensing platform for further simple and sensitive detection of some other aromatic ring compounds.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most widely used nitro-substituted aromatic explosive, 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) has resulted in many explosion hazards, environmental contaminations and human diseases such as toxic cataract, anemia, toxic hepatitis, spleen enlargement and so on [1]. Hence, it is momentous to establish rapid and sensitive devices and methods to detect TNT. Nowadays, a great many of methods, such as fluorescence [2], surface plasmon resonance [3], field-effect transistor [4] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [5], have been employed for TNT detection. But these methods are usually expensive, time-consuming and complicated in operation [6,7]. However, electrochemical method has been a promising approach for detecting TNT, due to its inner merits of high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and miniaturization [8–11]. The electrochemical reduction reaction of TNT is a three separate process with a close connection with the electron structure of the interfaces and surface chemistry of electrodes [12], which is majorly depended on the absorbability and catalytic performance of electrode materials. So, the performance of electrode modified materials is vital in TNT sensing. Some modified electrodes, such as the metal oxides and carbon-based materials modified electrodes, have already been employed in detecting TNT, but these modified electrodes usually have several shortcomings such as relatively low absorption property and catalytic effect for TNT or require complex preparation procedures [13].

As a kind of typical 2D layered material, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has caused comprehensive attention. Nowadays, MoS2 has been successfully used in various areas such as optoelectronics, energy storage and so on [14,15]. Beside these applications, on account of its distinctive sandwich-like structure, large surface area and application in surface modification, MoS2 has been widely applied in the area of electrochemical sensing [16–18]. Compared with sole MoS2, the MoS2-based composites possess enhanced properties. The surface modification can be an effective method to strengthen the electrochemical properties of the MoS2-based composites. Metal nanoparticles [19], carbon-based materials [20] and organic polymers [21] are usual materials to combine with MoS2 to obtain rich functions. Amid several synthetic methods, the electrochemical method was considered as a convenient method for preparing MoS2-based nanocomposites. Ji et al. fabricated a nonenzymatic glucose sensor based on Ni(OH)2/MoSx nanocomposite through a two-step electrodeposition [22]. Our team also synthesized free-standing ZnO nanosheets grown on 2D thin-layered MoS2 via electrodeposition towards DNA electrochemical sensing [23]. Besides metal and metal oxide, organic polymers, such as the poly (xanthurenic acid, Xa) and polyaniline (PANI), have also been successfully used to prepare MoS2-based nanocomposites. A PXa-MoS2 film was obtained by enhanced electropolymerization to realize highly sensitive detection of guanine and adenine [24]. The electropolymerization efficiency was obviously enhanced due to the physical adsorption between MoS2 and aromatic Xa, which accompanied with an increased electrochemical response of PXa. A dopamine (DA) sensor was fabricated based on the PANI-MoS2 nanocomposite, and exhibited excellent stability and sensitivity [25].

Poly(aminobenzenesulfonic acid, PABSA), overcomes several disadvantages of classic polyaniline (PANI), such as low water solubility and limited electrochemical activity in acidic solution [26–28], has been regarded as a prospective material in electrochemical sensing. For example, Chen et al. prepared electrochemical sensors based on poly(p-ABSA)-flavins for electroanalysis of NADH and NAD+ [29]. But until now, there is still no report about MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite for TNT detection.

In this paper, a thin-layered MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite was prepared via a simple electropolymerization procedure to fabricate an electrochemical TNT sensor. Due to a strong π-π interaction with the conjugated MoS2-PABSA, this nanocomposite obtained high absorbability, superior catalytic performance and exhibits excellent electrochemical sensing property towards aromatic ring compounds, such as TNT. This research will be meaningful for the development of sensitive MoS2-based electrochemical sensors.

All of the electrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Company, China) with a conventional three-electrode system, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) or modified GCE was used as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode. The measurement of pH was carried out on a PHS-25 digital pH meter (Shanghai Leici Factory, China). The prepared materials were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-6700F, JEOL, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM 2100, JEOL, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum (Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrophotometer, Bruker Company, Germany), and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) (Bruker D8 advanced X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation, operated at 40kV and 30mA, Bruker Company, Germany).

3-Aminobenzenesulfonic acid (m-ABSA, 98%) and bulk MoS2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) was bought from Bodi chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China), potassium ferrocyanide was obtained from Guangcheng chemical reagent company (Tianjin, China), 2,4,6-trinitromethylbenzene (TNT) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by mixing 0.1molL−1 NaH2PO4 and 0.1molL−1 Na2HPO4. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%) was bought from Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade or higher and aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water from an Aquapro ultrapure water system (Ever Young Enterprises Development. Co., Ltd., Chongqing, China).

Prior to the modification of GCE, bare GCE was polished with 0.3μm and 0.05μm α-Al2O3 separately to get a glossy surface. Then, it was rinsed by ultrapure water and washed in an ultrasonic cleaner. Later, the bare GCE was immersed into 5.0mmolL−1 m-ABSA solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl and electrochemical polymerized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) for 14 scan cycles with a scan range of −1.2–1.6V and sweep rate of 50mVs−1. The resulting electrode was named as PABSA/GCE.

Bulk MoS2 was dispersed in DMF and carried out a simple ultrasonic exfoliation route at room temperature for 5h to get a suspension of thin-layered MoS2 with a concentration of 1mgmL−1. Then 20μL of the above suspension was dripped on the tip of bare GCE and dried naturally to get MoS2 modified GCE, noted as MoS2/GCE. Using the same electropolymerization method as mentioned above, MoS2-PABSA/GCE was obtained by polymerizing m-ABSA on MoS2/GCE.

The CV parameters in 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution were as follows: the scan rate was 0.1Vs−1 and the scan range was −0.32–0.60V. The electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) parameters in 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution: the pulse amplitude was 5mV, the frequency range was 0.01–105 Hz, under open circuit condition.
The CV for self-signal characterization of the nanocomposite: The supporting electrolyte was 0.30molL−1 PBS (pH7.0). The scan range was −0.32–0.60V.

The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurement was carried out in 0.30molL−1 PBS (pH6.0), with a scan range from −0.9–−0.1V, pulse amplitude of 0.05V, pulse width of 0.05V and a pulse period of 2s.
All of above experiments were carried out under room temperature and all reported results were the mean value of three parallel measurements.

Fig. 1 shows the electropolymerization plots of MoS2-PABSA and sole PABSA. As shown in the curve a, blue line (sole PABSA), in the first cycle, an anodic peak and a cathodic peak emerged at 1.047V and −0.572V. Afterwards, during the second cycle, an anodic peak appeared at 0.509V and a cathodic peak emerged at 0.148V, which was similar to the previous report, manifesting the successful polymerization of ABSA and the formation of PABSA [30]. With the scanning cycles increasing, the peak current became larger. This illustrates that the polymer membrane is continually growing. The specific peaks of MoS2-PABSA/GCE (curve b, red line) were obviously larger than that of ABSA/GCE (curve a, blue line), which reveals that the existence of MoS2 can prompt the polymerization efficiency of ABSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials characterization of the MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A, C, and D are the SEM images of MoS2, PABSA, and MoS2-PABSA, respectively. The MoS2 (Fig. 2A) exhibits a lamellar structure and relatively smooth surface, which was further confirmed by the TEM image of MoS2 (Fig. 2B). Fig. 2C (sole PABSA) displays that the electrode surface was decorated with several spherical nanoparticles. Fig. 2D reflects that the morphology of MoS2-PABSA is particularly different from that of individual MoS2 and PABSA. The surface of MoS2-PABSA was uneven and showed many swellings. This special structure may be due to the specific “SMoS” structure of thin-layered MoS2 [18] and the electrostatic force between MoS2 and ABSA. It also indicates that MoS2 and PABSA have been successfully integrated through the electrochemical polymerization.

Fig. S1 is the FT-IR spectra of MoS2-PABSA. The peak at 1597cm−1 was corresponded to the CC stretching vibration of quinoid. The two vibrations at 1507cm−1 and 1481cm−1 were assigned to the benzenoid absorption. Characteristic band at 1104cm−1 was corresponded to CN stretching mode [31,32]. The peaks emerged at 1029, 1185 and 1246cm−1 were corresponded to the OSO groups vibration [31,33]. The peaks at 703 and 3443cm−1 were separately assigned to the SO and OH groups of the PABSA [31,34]. The peak emerged at 619cm−1 was corresponded to the MoS group [25,35].

Fig. S2 is the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of the MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite. The diffraction peaks at 14.3°, 32.6°, 39.5°, 50.1°, and 58.4° were respectively corresponded to the (002), (100), (103), (105) and (110) planes of MoS2 [21,35,36]. The diffraction peaks at 28.6° (020), 35.7° (120) and 38.8° (121) were assigned to the crystalline phase of PABSA [28,37]. Thus, the FT-IR and XRD result also confirmed the successful integration of MoS2 and PABSA.

The modified electrodes (MoS2/GCE, PABSA/GCE and MoS2-PABSA/GCE) were further characterized by electrochemical method in both [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution and PBS. Fig. 3A shows the CV responses of 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at these modified electrodes. It can be seen that both MoS2/GCE (curve a) and PABSA/GCE (curve b) displayed weak redox peak currents, indicating low electrical conductivity. However, after the synthesis of PABSA on the MoS2 modified electrode, the obtained MoS2-PABSA/GCE (curve c) showed much larger peak currents than MoS2/GCE and PABSA/GCE. It might be because that the combination of MoS2 of PABSA brought about larger specific surface area and higher electrical conductivity, thus the electron transfer was effectively promoted [35]. Fig. 3B is the Nyquist diagram recorded at different modified electrodes in 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution, it can be seen that the MoS2-PABSA/GCE (curve c) exhibited the smallest electron transfer resistance (corresponds to the diameter of the semicircles in Nyquist diagrams) [38]. It illustrates that the MoS2-PABSA/GCE exhibits excellent electrical conductivity, which also corresponds to the result of CV.

Fig. 3C displays the CV plots of these electrodes recorded in 0.30molL−1 PBS (pH7.0). Curve a shows no redox peak, indicating that MoS2/GCE has no redox activity in PBS (pH7.0). By contrast, a couple of redox peaks appeared in CV curves of PABSA/GCE (curve b) and MoS2-PABSA/GCE (curve c), which are the redox responses of PABSA under neutral conditions [39]. Moreover, the MoS2-PABSA/GCE possessed larger peak current than PABSA/GCE, which indicated that the existence of MoS2 improved electrochemical activity of PABSA effectively.

Fig. 3D shows DPV plots of 1.0ppm TNT recorded at different modified electrodes. As seen, there are three independent reduction peaks nearby −0.336V, −0.487V and −0.603V at all electrodes, which corresponded to the sequential reduction of three nitro groups of TNT (Fig. 3E): through a 6-electron transfer route, the two NO2 groups in ortho position were converted to NH2 groups successively, then the NO2 group located in the para position was reduced to a NH2 group [40,41]. It is clearly shown that the reduction signal at MoS2/GCE (curve a) is the lowest, demonstrating the poorest electrochemical catalytic effect. Besides, the PABSA/GCE (curve b) has larger TNT reduction signal, which may be due to the π-π stacking effect between TNT and PABSA. While at the MoS2-PABSA/GCE (curve c), the reduction peak current increased obviously, which indicated the MoS2-PABSA/GCE exhibited a synergetic catalytic effect towards TNT. Due to the existence of MoS2, the polymerization effect of PABSA was improved, as a result, the MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite gained abundant conjugate structure and large surface area, and the absorption efficiency could be increased through π-π stacking effect. Thus, MoS2-PABSA/GCE possessed an excellent catalytic effect for the reduction of TNT.

In order to obtain the optimal MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite, different polymerization cycles of PABSA had been investigated. Fig. 4 shows the CV and EIS plots of MoS2-PABSA/GCEs prepared with different polymerization cycles (selected 13, 14, 15, respectively) using 1.0mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1:1) as an indicator. It can be seen, when the polymerization cycles was 14 (curve c), the MoS2-PABSA/GCE showed the largest redox peak current (Fig. 4A) and the smallest resistance value (Fig. 4B). While the polymerization cycles reduced or increased, the peak currents decreased. This reveals that 14cycles is the best polymerization cycle.

Furthermore, the difference of polymerization cycles had an influence on the reduction signal of TNT, as shown in Fig. 4C. It shows that the MoS2-PABSA obtained from 14 polymerization cycles owned the largest peak current, indicating the best catalytic effect for the reduction of TNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH value on the reduction signal of TNT
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of detection solution had an influence on the electroreduction signal of TNT, as shown in Fig. 5 . With the pH value of PBS increasing, the TNT catalytic effect of MoS2-PABSA/GCE was obviously improved at first. When the pH value was 6.0 (curve c), the peak current reached the maximum. With the further increasing of the pH, the peak current decreased. This reveals that 6.0 is the best pH value, and others detection experiments were carried out under this pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of a series of concentration of TNT
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, a series of concentrations of TNT were detected at the MoS2-PABSA/GCE by DPV method, and the results were displayed in Fig. 6A. It can be seen that the peak current increased continuously with the TNT concentration increasing. The peak at −0.336V was chosen to investigate the sensitivity and detection limit for TNT detection, due to its sharpest shape [42]. Fig. 6B and C clearly reveal the relationship between the peak current and the concentration of TNT in the concentration ranging with five orders of magnitude (0.1–1000ppb). And it showed good linear relation in the concentration range of 0.1–200ppb with a linear regression equation Ip =0.0331C+1.1934 (R2 =0.9913), and the limit of detection was 0.043ppb (0.187nmolL−1). In comparison with some recently reports, the result shows a lower detection limit and a better linear range (Table. S1 in the supporting information). This result reveals that the MoS2-PABSA/GCE has excellent sensitivity and low detection limit for TNT electrochemical detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interferring ability of MoS2-PABSA and TNT detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Some easily reducible organic species and common water contaminants were used to evaluate the anti-interferring ability of MoS2-PABSA modified electrode for TNT detection [43]. It is observed that 50ppb excess of aniline [44], phenol and 500ppb Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and NO3 − [13] did not arouse visible influence on the DPV response of 5ppb TNT [45]. The result was due to the quite low reduction potential of TNT [45]. High concentrations of common surfactants, for instance, the sodium dodecyl sulfate (100ppb) caused an 11% diminution on the response signal of 5ppb TNT. Since in most water sample, the concentration of surfactants is usually at a lower level, such interferences are very feeble in practice [43].

In order to evaluate the suitability of the MoS2-PABSA modified electrode for the detection of TNT in a nature water sample, a recovery measurement was carried out in untreated tap water samples contaminated with 10ppb TNT. The recovery for the 5 parallel measurements was ranging from 97.4% to 101%, which also demonstrated the good practicality and high reproducibility of the MoS2-PABSA modified electrode [43,46]. Furthermore, the stability was evaluated by comparing the electrocatalytic performances of the prepared MoS2-PABSA modified electrode before and after seven days [46]. The signal can be maintained at 94%. These results indicate that the MoS2-PABSA modified electrode is suitable for real TNT sample determination.

In summary, MoS2-PABSA nanocomposite was successfully obtained through a simple electrosynthesis procedure. The results from surface and electrochemical characterization revealed that this nanocomposite combined each advantage of MoS2 and PABSA. Based on this material, a catalytic platform showing outstanding detection of molecules with conjugate structures was established. As a model, highly sensitive detection of TNT was realized. With these remarkable advantages, this work could inspire researchers to exploit the widespread electrochemical applications of the emerging MoS2-based nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275084, 41476083, 21675092, 21422504), Outstanding Youth Foundation of Shandong Province (JQ201406), Outstanding Adult-Young Scientific Research Encouraging Foundation of Shandong Province (No. BS2012CL013) and the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province, China (ts20110829).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2016.09.009.

A new molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)-based electrochemical sensor for monitoring 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) in natural waters and soil samples
Remote electrochemical sensor for monitoring TNT in natural waters
Simple and sensitive fluorescent and electrochemical trinitrotoluene sensors based on aqueous carbon dots
